text: Hello and welcome to this video article presenting the Edinburgh Posterior Approach to Vax Pneumonectomy.
What we're looking at here is the CT scan of a seven-year-old lady presented with an incidental finding of a left upper lobe lesion on chest x-ray.
PET CT revealed this fifty by thirty millimetre T-two-B-N-naught-M-naught lesion which as you can see from the scan is very close to the aorta and pulmonary artery.
The further concern was that the tumour was invading at the level of the orifice of the left upper lobe.

Based on the imaging available and observations at the time of surgery, it was decided to attempt a pneumonectomy as opposed to a bronchovascular sleeve where the risk of a positive resection margin would be high.
In this particular case, it was apparent from the outset that the tumour was adhered to the aorta as a result of tumour spread.
The surgeon dissects into the sub-amphetitial plane to remove any diseased mediastinal pleura, carefully separating the pleura from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon clips and divides the superior intercostal vein. The vagus nerve is also divided due to the specific requirements of this case.
The surgeon examines the posterior hilum using a blunt instrument to reflect the pleura, assessing for involvement of the left bronchus, pulmonary artery, and the recurrent laryngeal nerve.
The surgeon focuses on the bronchus, preparing to assess and manipulate this structure during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses diathermy attached to surgical shears to carefully remove the pleura overlying the thymus, exposing the underlying structures.
The surgeon skeletonizes the bronchus using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques, carefully separating the bronchus from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon exposes the station five lymph nodes to the right of the bronchus and the station seven lymph node to the left of the bronchus, highlighting their anatomical positions in relation to the bronchus.
In this particular case, the station seven node was sent for a frozen section to ensure there was no metastatic involvement of the N-II lymph nodes.
The return pathology was negative for disease and another benefit of removing the node at this stage is the resultant clearance of the subcarinal space.
The surgeon carefully passes a right-angled Roberts clamp anterior to the bronchus, ensuring the pulmonary artery is not damaged during the maneuver.
The surgeon places a vascular sling into the jaws of the right-angled Roberts clamp, using it to retract and enhance the view of the proximal bronchus.
The surgeon uses node holding forceps to remove the station five lymph nodes, which are now clearly visible due to the improved visualization from the previous maneuver.
The surgeon focuses on the inferior and anterior hilum, using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to carefully dissect the inferior pulmonary ligament.
The surgeon carefully dissects the pleura posterior to the phrenic nerve, allowing for clear visualization and assessment of the pulmonary vein, which appears free from disease.

The surgeon passes the Roberts clamp behind the superior pulmonary vein, which is then secured using a TAN-Forty-Five tri-stapler inserted through the posterior port and fired to complete the pneumonectomy.
The surgeon retracts the bronchus using a vascular sling, positioning it to facilitate the resection of the bronchus as close to the carina as possible.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers to avoid the pulmonary artery, which is visibly positioned directly anterior to the bronchus during the dissection.
The surgeon passes a stapler through the anterior port and fires it, completing the stapling of the targeted structure.
After dividing the bronchus, the surgeon gains a clear view of the pulmonary artery. The Roberts clamp is passed behind the artery to create a passageway for the Tan-Forty-Five Tri-Stapler.
The surgeon passes the stapler through the anterior utility port, positioning it flush against the pulmonary artery before firing to ensure precise separation.
The surgeon gently rotates the bronchus back and forth using a vascular sling to facilitate its retraction and positioning for division near the carina.
The surgeon closes the stapler around the targeted tissue and fires it, ensuring the pulmonary artery remains unobstructed during the procedure.
The surgeon prepares to take the final vest, the inferior pulmonary vein, using a surgical stapler passed through the anterior port.
In this particular case this was left to last to prevent vascular engorgement which can often hinder progress.
The Tan-Forty-Five tri stapler is maneuvered into position, aligning just past either side of the vein. The surgeon gently maneuvers the stapler, closes it, and then fires to secure the tissue.
The surgeon completes the visualization of the hilar structures, ensuring clear exposure of these anatomical features.
The surgeon achieves haemostasis, ensuring that bleeding is controlled in the surgical field following the firing of the stapler.
The specimen is placed into a plastic bag prior to removal, a technique used to prevent contamination and seeding in the wound.
After the stapler maneuver, the surgeon checks the bronchus for air leaks, places a paravertebral catheter adjacent to the sympathetic chain, and inserts a chest drain into the thoracic cavity.

The video shows an eight-centimeter utility port and its orientation relative to other incisions. A post-operative day one chest X-ray is displayed, indicating the satisfactory position of the mediastinum.
The final pathology revealed a T-IV-N-I-M-naught squamous cell carcinoma.
This video was produced in the Royal Infirmary of Edinburgh in the Cardiothoracic Unit and we thank you for your viewing of our production.
